title: Making a decorative bookplate
subtitle: Designing and tipping in a hand-drawn ex libris label
excerpt: A bookplate is a small printed or drawn label pasted inside the front board of a book to identify its owner. The tradition of hand-drawn bookplates stretches from the 15th century. This tutorial covers designing a simple ex libris label, printing or drawing multiple copies, and tipping one into a newly bound book.
sourceNotes: The bookplate tradition is documented in E. Almack, Book-Plates (1897), a public-domain survey of the English ex-libris from the 15th to 19th centuries, available via Internet Archive. The tipping-in method follows standard bookbinding practice.

glossaryTerms.definition: A decorative label pasted to the inside front board of a book, traditionally inscribed Ex Libris (from the library of) followed by the owner's name.
Attaching a leaf into a book by applying adhesive to a narrow strip along one edge only, so the leaf turns as a free flap.

content.text: is a small label pasted inside the front board of a book, naming its owner. The tradition goes back to the 15th century. It reached its peak in the late 19th century when artists produced elaborate engraved and woodcut ex libris designs. A hand-drawn bookplate for a hand-bound book continues that same tradition.

items.name: Cartridge paper, 130 gsm
items.notes: For the bookplate itself. A heavier paper (150 to 180 gsm) gives a more substantial feel.

items.name: PVA, acid-free
items.notes: For pasting the bookplate into the book.

items.name: Paste brush, 50 mm flat (or a smaller brush)
items.notes: For applying adhesive evenly to the back of the bookplate.

items.name: Bone folder
items.notes: For pressing the bookplate flat after positioning.

items.name: Craft scissors
items.notes: For cutting bookplates to size from a larger sheet.

content.text: A traditional bookplate has a border, a central image or motif, and the words "Ex Libris" followed by the owner's name. Classic motifs include an open book, a candle, a botanical illustration, a coat of arms, or a quotation. Keep the design to the size of a playing card or smaller: a bookplate that fills the whole front board looks more like a label than an embellishment. Draw or print the design, then transfer it to the bookplate paper.

Apply an even coat of diluted PVA to the full back of the bookplate. Position it in the upper left corner of the front paste-down, 10 mm from the head and 10 mm from the spine edge. Press with a bone folder from the centre outward to remove air bubbles. Place waxed paper over the bookplate and close the book. Press under weight for 30 minutes.

rather than fully pasted. Apply adhesive to a 5 mm strip along the left edge of the bookplate only. This allows the plate to turn as a flap, which is useful when the bookplate carries additional information on its reverse or when the binding requires reversible attachment. Tipped plates are less secure in daily use than fully pasted ones.

items.cause: The adhesive was applied too thickly, introducing excess moisture.
items.fix: Use a thinner coat of PVA. Diluting PVA with 20% water gives a coat thin enough to prevent cockling on light papers.

items.symptom: The bookplate sits at an angle in the book
items.cause: It was placed before the adhesive became slightly tacky, allowing it to slide.
items.fix: Wait 20 to 30 seconds after applying adhesive before placing, so the surface has a slight grip.
